title: Billy Vickers
content:
آ آ آ آ No song is this of leaf and bird,
آ آ آ آ And gracious waters flowing;
آ آ آ آ Iï؟½m sick at heart, for I have heard
آ آ آ آ Big Billy Vickers ï؟½blowingï؟½.

آ آ آ آ Heï؟½d never take a leading place
آ آ آ آ In chambers legislative:
آ آ آ آ This booby with the vacant face
آ آ آ آ This hoddy-doddy native!

آ آ آ آ Indeed, Iï؟½m forced to say aside,
آ آ آ آ To you, O reader, solely,
آ آ آ آ He only wants the horns and hide
آ آ آ آ To be a bullock wholly.

آ آ آ آ But, like all noodles, he is vain;
آ آ آ آ And when his tongue is wagging,
آ آ آ آ I feel inclined to copy Cain,
آ آ آ آ And ï؟½dropï؟½ him for his bragging.

آ آ آ آ He, being Bush-bred, stands, of course,
آ آ آ آ Six feet his dirty socks in;
آ آ آ آ His lingo is confined to horse
آ آ آ آ And plough, and pig and oxen.

آ آ آ آ Two years ago heï؟½d less to say
آ آ آ آ Within his little circuit;
آ آ آ آ But now he has, besides a dray,
آ آ آ آ A team of twelve to work it.

آ آ آ آ No wonder is it that he feels
آ آ آ آ Inclined to clack and rattle
آ آ آ آ About his bullocks and his wheels
آ آ آ آ He owns a dozen cattle.

آ آ آ آ In short, to be exact and blunt,
آ آ آ آ In his own estimation
آ آ آ آ Heï؟½s ï؟½out and outï؟½ the head and front
آ آ آ آ Top-sawyer of creation!

آ آ آ آ For, mark me, he can ï؟½sit a buckï؟½
آ آ آ آ For hours and hours together;
آ آ آ آ And never horse has had the luck
آ آ آ آ To pitch him from the leather.

آ آ آ آ If ever he should have a ï؟½spillï؟½
آ آ آ آ Upon the grass or gravel,
آ آ آ آ Be sure of this, the saddle will
آ آ آ آ With Billy Vickers travel.

آ آ آ آ At punching oxen you may guess
آ آ آ آ Thereï؟½s nothing out can ï؟½campï؟½ him:
آ آ آ آ He has, in fact, the slouch and dress
آ آ آ آ Which bullock-driver stamp him.

آ آ آ آ I do not mean to give offence,
آ آ آ آ But I have vainly striven
آ آ آ آ To ferret out the difference
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Twixt driver and the driven.

آ آ آ آ Of course, the statements herein made
آ آ آ آ In every other stanza
آ آ آ آ Are Billyï؟½s own; and Iï؟½m afraid
آ آ آ آ Theyï؟½re stark extravaganza.

آ آ آ آ I feel constrained to treat as trash
آ آ آ آ His noisy fiddle-faddle
آ آ آ آ About his doings with the lash,
آ آ آ آ His feats upon the saddle.

آ آ آ آ But grant he ï؟½knows his way aboutï؟½,
آ آ آ آ Or grant that he is silly,
آ آ آ آ There cannot be the slightest doubt
آ آ آ آ Of Billyï؟½s faith in Billy.

آ آ آ آ Of all the doings of the day
آ آ آ آ His ignorance is utter;
آ آ آ آ But he can quote the price of hay,
آ آ آ آ The current rate of butter.

آ آ آ آ His notions of our leading men
آ آ آ آ Are mixed and misty very:
آ آ آ آ He knows a cochin-china hen
آ آ آ آ He never speaks of Berry.

آ آ آ آ As youï؟½ll assume, he hasnï؟½t heard
آ آ آ آ Of Madame Pattiï؟½s singing;
آ آ آ آ But I will stake my solemn word
آ آ آ آ He knows what maize is bringing.

آ آ آ آ Surrounded by majestic peaks,
آ آ آ آ By lordly mountain ranges,
آ آ آ آ Where highest voice of thunder speaks
آ آ آ آ His aspect never changes.

آ آ آ آ The grand Pacific there beyond
آ آ آ آ His dirty hut is glowing:
آ آ آ آ He only sees a big salt pond,
آ آ آ آ Oï؟½er which his grain is going.

آ آ آ آ The sea that covers half the sphere,
آ آ آ آ With all its stately speeches,
آ آ آ آ Is held by Bill to be a mere
آ آ آ آ Broad highway for his peaches.

آ آ آ آ Through Natureï؟½s splendid temples he
آ آ آ آ Plods, under mountains hoary;
آ آ آ آ But he has not the eyes to see
آ آ آ آ Their grandeur and their glory.

آ آ آ آ A bullock in a bipedï؟½s boot,
آ آ آ آ I iterate, is Billy!
آ آ آ آ He crushes with a careless foot
آ آ آ آ The touching water-lily.

آ آ آ آ Iï؟½ve said enough Iï؟½ll let him go!
آ آ آ آ If he could read these verses,
آ آ آ آ Heï؟½d pepper me for hours, I know,
آ آ آ آ With his peculiar curses.

آ آ آ آ But this is sure, heï؟½ll never change
آ آ آ آ His manners loud and flashy,
آ آ آ آ Nor learn with neatness to arrange
آ آ آ آ His clothing, cheap and trashy.

آ آ آ آ Like other louts, heï؟½ll jog along,
آ آ آ آ And swig at shanty liquors,
آ آ آ آ And chew and spit. Here ends the song
آ آ آ آ Of Mr. Billy Vickers.
meaning: About: Henry Clarence Kendall was an important nineteenth century Australian poet.
